q: State Epictetus's core principle about what is and is not 'up to us', with a verbatim quote from the corpus.
final:
Epictetus's core principle about what is and is not "up to us" is encapsulated in the **dichotomy of control**. According to this principle, some things are within our power (or "up to us"), while others are not. Specifically:

- **Up to us (within our power)**: Our judgments, desires, aversions, impulses, and actions in general—what we actively choose or control.
- **Not up to us (outside our power)**: Our body, property, reputation, and external circumstances—things we do not control.

The principle is stated verbatim in the corpus as follows:

> **"Of things some are in our power, and others are not. In our power are opinion ([Greek: *hupolaepsis*]), movement towards a thing ([Greek: *hormae*]), desire, aversion ([Greek: *echchlisis*]), turning from a thing; and in a word, whatever are our acts. Not in our power are the body, property, reputation, offices (magisterial power), and in a word, whatever are not our own acts."**

This principle is further clarified as:
> **"The things in our power are by nature free, not subject to restraint or hindrance; but the things not in our power are weak, slavish, subject to restraint, in the power of others."**

tool_calls.name: federated_note_html
tool_calls.result_preview:
Дихотомия контроля (τὰ ἐφ' ἡμῖν)

Первое и главное деление всей системы, открывающее Мануал. Мир разрезан надвое:
то, что в нашей власти (суждение, побуждение, желание, уклонение — «наши
собственные действия»), и то, что не в нашей власти (тело, имущество,
репутация, должности). Первое по природе свободно и не встречает препятствий;
второе слабо, рабственно, подвластно другим.
